coredata.dc:title: Modulation of fluorescence radiation for ZnCdS/CdSe quantum dots by graphene at room temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dongwen
Wang, Li
Su, Xueqiong
Pan, Yong
Li, Shufeng
Han, Xiaowei
Wang, Yimeng
coredata.dc:description: We investigate the photofluorescence (PL) of ZnCdS/CdSe quantum dots (QDs) film by modulating with graphene QDs. The modulated spectra show a blue shift from 539.86 nm to 534.76 nm. At the same time, the intensity of fluorescence spectrum after modulation is obviously higher than that without modulation. Femtosecond transient absorption results of the QDs film show the addition of graphene QDs can effectively increase the lifetime of photons and provide more energy levels. In addition, a simple resonator structure of the capillary microcavity is designed based on the principle of laser generation and finally obtained at 531.9 nm laser output in the fluorescence peak of the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs in the experimental, which indicates that the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs laser is feasible. The results will also play an important role in the research and development of other QDs lasers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnCdS/CdSe quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots
Core-shell structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) ZnCdS/CdSe QDs film dropping process (b) Capillary microcavity preparation process, microcavity schematic diagram and photo physical diagram.

ZnCdS/CdSe QDs TEM diagram (a) Counting region of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs size distribution (b) One sixteenth of the region in a-graph (c) Selected observation areas (d) The enlargement of the block part in the c-graph (e) The enlargement of the block part in the d-graph (f) The enlargement of the block part in the e-graph.
Column diagram of the size distribution of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs.
(a) XRD pattern of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs (b) Raman pattern of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs without graphene and with a small amount of graphene and the illustration is the calculated optical band gap (b) Fluorescence spectra of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs without or with a small amount of graphene film.

(a) 520 nm femtosecond transient absorption contrast image with different delays (b) normalized contrast image of 10 ps delay of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs without adding or adding a small amount of graphene QDs.
Laser contrast diagrams produced by ZnCdS/CdSe QDs with (a) no graphene (b) less graphene (c) Excess graphene (d) Laser contrast spectra before and after co-modulation by two methods.

head.ce:title: Modulation of fluorescence radiation for ZnCdS/CdSe quantum dots by graphene at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Applied Sciences, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Applied Sciences, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Semiconductor Lighting Display, School of Electrical & Electronic Engineering, Singapore 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Semiconductor Lighting Display

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Semiconductor Lighting Display, School of Electrical & Electronic Engineering, Singapore 639798, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigate the photofluorescence (PL) of ZnCdS/CdSe quantum dots (QDs) film by modulating with graphene QDs. The modulated spectra show a blue shift from 539.86 nm to 534.76 nm. At the same time, the intensity of fluorescence spectrum after modulation is obviously higher than that without modulation. Femtosecond transient absorption results of the QDs film show the addition of graphene QDs can effectively increase the lifetime of photons and provide more energy levels. In addition, a simple resonator structure of the capillary microcavity is designed based on the principle of laser generation and finally obtained at 531.9 nm laser output in the fluorescence peak of the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs in the experimental, which indicates that the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs laser is feasible. The results will also play an important role in the research and development of other QDs lasers.

ce:section.ce:para: In the year of 1993, uniformly dispersed Cd-based QDs are published [1–5]. A great deal of research has been conducted on the QDs prepared by organic metal and chalcogenides, and the research on these QDs has developed rapidly [6–8]. As a new type of zero-dimensional material, QDs has its unique advantages and characteristics [9–12]. Because of its quantum size, it has unique quantum size effects, quantum confinement effects, and surface effects [13,14]. These features make QDs have a wide range of illumination, adjustable wavelength, high efficiency, and stable illumination. Therefore, it has many advantages in some applications area and it is widely used in optical display. Biomedical technology and other fields [15–20]. Among the QDs formed by many organic metal and chalcogenides, the fluorescence adjustable range of CdSe QDs can cover the entire visible light range, so CdSe QDs have a wide range of applications [21]. However, due to the surface effect of CdSe QDs, it has a large specific surface area, and there are numerous uncoordinated atoms on the surface. As the size of QDs decreases, both surface defects and surface dangling bonds increase, which affect the fluorescence efficiency of CdSe QDs [22–26]. Therefore, the passivation treatment of core-shell structure is introduced to the surface of CdSe QDs to alleviate this situation, and the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs are obtained. ZnCdS/CdSe QDs is anti-I core-shell structures [27–30]. Its characteristic is that the band gap of the core material is larger than that of the shell material [31]. By adjusting the thickness of the shell material, the electrons and holes can be partially or completely confined to the shell. Thus, realizing the adjustment of the overall fluorescence wavelength of the material [32–37]. This technology has been relatively mature [38,39]. However, this adjustment method can only be limited to the preparation process of QDs, and relatively little research has been done on the preparation of QDs prepared [40]. On the other hand, the current research and application of QDs are mostly concentrated in the fluorescence part of the field, and the research on laser generation of QDs is relatively less. At the same time, it is difficult for lasers to achieve continuous conversion of emission wavelengths. Combining with the fluorescence characteristics of QDs, lasers made by QDs will have excellent characteristics that traditional lasers do not possess.

In this research, the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs are selected for experiment, which will combine with various excellent properties of graphene, such as stability, adjustable band gap, optical and high transparency for realizing the fluorescence modulation of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs. In addition, a glass capillary resonator is designed to study the laser output characteristics of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs. The modulation result of the resonant cavity and graphene for ZnCdS/CdSe QDs laser is obtained. The results show that a laser peak occurs near 531 nm, and the feasibility of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs laser is proved in the experiments.

The ZnCdS/CdSe QDs of the anti-I-type core-shell structure is used in the experiment. The surface ligand is oleate, the solvent is chloroform, the QDs concentration is 2 mg/ml, and a small amount of graphene QDs. Fig. 1 (a) shows a process of preparing thin film samples by dropping method. Quartz glass substrates with the size of 2 cm × 2 cm are selected, and all four quartz substrates were dripped with 100 μl QDs solution and steam-drying. In the same way, a small amount (10 μl), an appropriate amount (30 μl) and an excess (60 μl) of graphene QDs solution are dropped on the other three samples, and the solvent is evaporated to prepare for the experiment. Because of the same method, Fig. 1(a) gives only one schematic diagram of the process of dropping QDs. Fig. 1(b) shows a flow chart for sample preparation of capillary microcavity, a schematic diagram of the microcavity structure and a physical photo diagram of the sample. Two kinds of glass capillary tubes with diameters of 0.3 mm and 0.5 mm respectively are selected. Two sizes are placed in the QDs solution for siphoning, and the other end is sealed and taken out. The solvent is evaporated and left for experimental standby, as showed in Fig. 1(b). The remaining capillaries are made by the same method during the experiment, but the operation is performed immediately after siphoning to complete the experimental test with solvent. QDs are inhaled into capillaries by siphon phenomenon. When the solvent of QDs is evaporated, two layers of coating consisting of QDs are formed on the inner and outer walls of the capillaries. Formed a ring sandwich structure, and because the surface of the capillary itself is relatively smooth, it satisfies the principle of the mirror. When the excitation light vertical capillary illuminates the QDs from the side, and the QDs plating on the inner and outer walls emits light, the light is oscillated and transmitted in the capillary wall between the two plating layers. This is a mode similar to echo wall transmission. After numerous oscillations, it is finally emitted from the end face of the capillary, and then the exit light of the capillary end face is measured, and the analysis is concluded. For the comprehensiveness of the experiment, the test is carried out before and after evaporation of the solvent, and the microcavity schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 1(b). Although the inner and outer wall QDs plating structure cannot be formed, some of the light emitted by the QDs satisfies the total reflection condition, and the effect is also produced since the refractive index of the QDs solvent is greater than the refractive index of the air. The test data of the sample are analyzed and the experimental results are obtained.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs are shown in the Fig. 2 . To determine the core-shell diameter of QDs, Fig. 2(a) is selected to count the size distribution of QDs, which is divided into 16 regions of 4 × 4 to improve the counting accuracy. Fig. 2(b) is one of the regions of Fig. 2(a). We counted each region individually to get the statistical result of the whole region. Finally, the result of Fig. 3 is obtained by counting 2192. We can clearly see that the number of QDs whose diameter is between 5.5 nm and 6.5 nm accounts for 87.68%, and the other sizes are much smaller than this proportion. Therefore, it can be concluded that the QDs are evenly distributed on the copper network with diameters ranging from 5.5 nm to 6.5 nm. They are monodispersed spherical particles, which meet the requirements of QDs and do not cluster. Fig. 2(c) to (f) are step-by-step enlargements of QDs in the order shown in the figure. The core-shell structure can be clearly seen from the QDs in Fig. 2(f). Shell part of CdSe is between 3 nm and 4 nm in size. Since the luminescent part of the anti-I core-shell structure is the shell, all QDs exhibit the luminescent range of 450–600 nm. The results are consistent with the experimental results of optical measurement.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs. The acquired patterns were compared with standard library cards in the JCPDS database, including ZnCdS (JCPDS 40-0836) [41], CdSe (JCPDS 02-0330) [42]. The diffraction peaks positioned at 2θ values 25.54°, 27.27° and 28.18° match well with the respective, (1 0 0), (0 0 2) and (1 0 1) crystal planes of the hexagonal cadmium Cd7.23Zn2.27S10 according to the standard JCPDS data card NO. 40-0836. The low intense peaks corresponding to (1 1 0) planes is also present in the spectrum. There are several strong peaks (1 1 1), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) sphalerite structure positioned at 2θ values 27.13°, 45° and 53.13°. The peak position of the hexagonal structure representing Cd7.23Zn2.27S10 marked by a blue solar icon. There is also the cubic structure representing CdSe marked with green stars, and no other miscellaneous phase is found. The appearance of broad peaks indicates that the samples belong to the amorphous structure, which conforms to the structural characteristics of QDs.

To demonstrate the formation of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs nanostructures as expected, we used RS spectroscopy. Fig. 4(b) shows the Raman spectra of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs with different quantity of graphene. For CdSe Shell, the spectrum exhibits one peak at 221 cm−1 associated with the longitudinal optical (LO) phonon (named LOCdS). There are two mode at 375 cm−1 and 446 cm−1, which are assigned to the LO phonon mode of the CdS (LOCdS) and ZnCdS (LOZnCdS). For TO modes, their intensity is usually very weak in comparison with that of LO [43]. In this work, we shall ignore the assessment of TO modes. We can see that no matter how many graphene QDs are added, there is no effect on Raman spectra. That is to say, the addition of graphene QDs does not affect the bonding of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs. QDs, nor does it affect the structure of QDs.

The comparison of the absorption spectra of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs and doping a small amount of graphene QDs in the wavelength range of 300–800 nm is shown in Fig. 5 (a). From Fig. 5(a) can be seen that the addition of graphene QDs can modulate the absorption spectrum of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs. Prior to 410 nm, the addition of graphene QDs reduced the absorption spectrum of pure QDs, but after 410 nm, the addition of graphene QDs increased the absorption spectrum of pure QDs. It is concluded that graphene inhibits the absorption in ultraviolet region of QDs and promotes the absorption in visible region at the same time. The reason for this phenomenon is that the barrier of graphene will result in the decrease of the contact probability between the excitation light and the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs quantum dots as graphene is added to quantum dots, so the absorption rate in the ultraviolet band will be reduced. However, only a small amount of graphene is added to the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs quantum dots, so the reduction is not very large. In the visible band, the absorption of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs quantum dots is relatively small. Meanwhile, graphene has a strong absorption effect on visible light, so the absorption rate of visible spectrum increases, which indicate that graphene plays a certain modulation role. Using the absorption spectrum obtained in Fig. 5(a), the corresponding optical band gap can be calculated, and the results are shown in the Inset Fig. 5(a). The optical band gap is obviously reduced after adding graphene, indicating the graphene participates in the energy level transition process. The reduction of the band gap reveals that the addition of graphene makes the graphene provide a lower energy level during the transition, which makes the transition easier and shows a certain modulation role of the graphene. Fig. 5(b) is a comparison of fluorescence spectra of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs and a small amount of graphene QDs in the wavelength range of 450–600 nm. It can be clearly seen in Fig. 5(b) that the intensity of the fluorescence is greatly improved, and the peak position of the modulated fluorescence spectrum shows blue shift from 539.86 nm to 534.76 nm. The phenomenon indicates that graphene QDs participate in the transition of energy levels, making it easier for electrons to transition to the upper energy level. When the electron transitions to the upper level, it spontaneously jumps to the nearby graphene level, and finally returns to the ground state and radiates the photon, which causes the blue shift in the experiment.

To verify the charge transfer process of QDs, we performed femtosecond transient absorption (TA) measurements on selective photoexcitation (at 400 nm) of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs, as shown in Fig. 6 . All of TA measurements are made under the same experimental conditions at low pump throughput (average exciton number per particle <N> is _0.15) to ensure negligible multi-exciton formation probability. Fig. 6(a) shows that the TA spectrum of the ZnCdS/CdSe QDs is characterized by a broad negative signal (bleaching) with a peak around 520 nm matching the first exciton band of the QDs. The valley values of 1 ps to 500 ps are different, which illustrates that there are many energy levels near the main energy level. After the electron transit to the conduction band, it spontaneously transits to the nearby energy level, so the photons with different lifetimes radiate correspond to different wavelengths. Fig. 6(b) is a comparison of bleaching signals produced by ZnCdS/CdSe QDs and the addition of a small amount of graphene QDs. It is found that the recovery of the bleaching signal is slow. However, in the presence of graphene QDs, although the spectral characteristics remain nearly the same, the bleaching amplitude is significantly reduced. This indicates a decrease in photoexcited carriers, and the recovery of the bleaching signal becomes faster when a small amount of graphene is added. Since exciton bleaching in ZnCdS/CdSe QDs is mainly controlled by electrokinetics, excitation at 400 nm (which is similar to the band gap of QDs calculated according to the band gap obtained above) produces electrons with edge states.

To achieve laser output, the laser structure was designed as well as manufactured a simple laser oscillation to conduct experiments. Fig. 7 (a) shows the laser spectra of pure QDs obtained by using microcavity structure at different laser power. The selected wavelength spectrum is in the range of 450–600 nm. Compared with the results of Fig. 5, Fig. 7(a) shows a trend of laser peaks at the top of the spectrum obtained by the microcavity. The illustration in Fig. 7(a) shows the spectral details of the region. This result makes the above conclusion more obvious and shows the trend peak of the laser near 533 nm. However, modulation results of the microcavity are not satisfactory. Considering the excellent modulation effect of graphene shown above, a small amount and an excess of graphene were added to the QDs in the subsequent experiments. At the same time, the microcavity was used to carry out the experiment again. The results are presented in Fig. 7(b) and (c). The results of Fig. 7(b) are consistent with the experimental assumption. There are obvious narrow pulse width peaks at the top of the spectrum. The details of the peaks are enlarged illustrations, and the peaks are located near 531 nm. In addition, it can be noted that when the excitation light is 5 mW, the spectral line only shows a single fluorescence peak, and there is no laser peak. The laser peak appears on the fluorescence peak as the power increases to 10 mW, and then increasing with the power continuously. This phenomenon indicates that the emergence of laser peaks requires threshold, and with the increase of excitation energy, the peak intensity increases gradually, and the pulse width of the peak is only a few nanometers, which conforms to the conditions and characteristics of laser peak output. Thus, the peak obtained in the experiment belongs to the laser peak. Compared with the peak position in Fig. 7(a), the experimental results are consistent with Fig. 5(b). By adding a small amount of graphene QDs, the laser peak Laser peak moving towards short wave (blue-shifts). Fig. 7(c) is the experimental result of adding excessive graphene, which is in the above image disappear. And no corresponding peaks are found through the details of the illustrations. The results show that the addition of a small amount of graphene has a positive modulation effect on the laser emission of QDs, but the addition of excessive graphene has a negative modulation effect on the QDs. From the Fig. 7(a), the emission after modulation are obviously better than those without modulation. At the same time, a sharp laser peak appears at 531.9 nm on the fluorescence peak. The experimental results show that the two methods in the experiment are beneficial to the laser generation. These findings show for the first time that graphene has a certain modulation effect on ZnCdS/CdSe QDs fluorescence. In addition, experimental results improve the feasibility of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs lasers.

In this paper, a significant modulation effect of graphene QDs on the fluorescence of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs have been proved. Fluorescence emission of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs can be enhanced by doping appropriate amount of graphene QDs. It is noteworthy that graphene QDs enhance the laser intensity of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs at 531.9 nm. The experimental results are consistent with the core-shell structure theory proposed. Sharp laser peaks were obtained by co-modulation of graphene QDs and capillary micro-resonators. Therefore, it is completely feasible to obtain laser light of ZnCdS/CdSe QDs by using this method. These results play an important role in the tunable quantum lasers.

Dongwen Gao: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Li Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xueqiong Su: Conceptualization. Yong Pan: Methodology, Investigation. Shufeng Li: Data curation. Xiaowei Han: Visualization. Yimeng Wang: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China under Youth Science Foundation Project (NSFC-61805005).
